scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is discussing the legal restorations in Athens following the democratic return after the rule of the Thirty Tyrants.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech reflects on the immediate aftermath of the democratic restoration (-403) and the settlement of property claims, delivered around -397 to -395.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly names the 'law of Aegina' as the governing statute for the will because the parties were residents there.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech was delivered during the trial in Aegina around -390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates cites the Aeginetan law requiring common civic status for adoption.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech is dated to Isocrates' early career, specifically the litigation regarding Thrasylochus's estate around -390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The speaker invokes the laws of Aegina which permit testamentary adoption for childless men to preserve the household.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is part of the Aegineticus litigation dated to approximately -390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The legal standing of inheritance for legitimate children vs. others is being argued in an Aeginetan court.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The litigation occurred in the late 390s or early 380s BCE; -390 is a standard estimate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The case concerns the exclusion of children from unrecognized unions under Aeginetan probate law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The case is contemporary to Isocrates' activity as a logographer in the early 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is describing the idealised or historical Egyptian social order under the mythical king Busiris.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Isocrates' Busiris is an encomium written around -388 BCE discussing Egyptian institutions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text details the economic privileges of the Egyptian priestly caste as established by Egyptian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Published during Isocrates' floruit, specifically ca. -388 BCE as a rhetorical exercise regarding Egyptian customs.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates explicitly compares Egyptian laws to the well-known Spartan restrictions on military travel abroad.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text dates the comparison to Isocrates' floruit (-388); the Spartan customs themselves were contemporary to him.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is describing the functional division of labour and property protections in Egypt.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Written as part of the Busiris around -388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the Egyptian law of fixed hereditary occupations.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Isocrates describes these as longstanding Egyptian laws in his work dated ca. -388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the political structure of the Egyptian state where seniors hold authority.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Consistent with the date of the Busiris, ca. -388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is exercising the right of an Athenian citizen to address the Panhellenic crowd and the Athenian assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Panegyricus was published around -380 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is praising the Athenian system's provision of funds and refuge for its citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated in the Panegyricus, published in -380 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates reflects the common Greek and specifically Athenian view of slaves as household property subject to mastery.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Published in -380 BCE as part of Isocrates' political advocacy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text discusses the exclusion of 'aliens' (metics) from the rights of native birthright citizens in Greek poleis, using Athens' framework.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: From the Panegyricus, ca. -380 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the exclusion of barbarians from the Eleusinian Mysteries, an Athenian religious institution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Isocrates describes this as a contemporary practice in -380 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates refers to the general Greek norm where rhetorical skill provides political power across various city-states.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: From the Panegyricus, published in -380 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is describing the virtues and norms of 'private life' within a democratic Greek city-state, primarily Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Written ca. -374 BCE as advice to the king of Cyprus, contrasting monarchy with the Athenian private life he knew.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates is writing to Nicocles, the king of Salamis in Cyprus, describing the internal social conditions of a monarchy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the early reign of Nicocles, ca. -374 BCE.
